coredata.dc:title: A rotating paper-based microfluidic sensor array combining Michael acceptors and carbon quantum dots for discrimination of biothiols
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Liang
Mei, Xuecui
Peng, Zhengchun
Liu, Jiang
Yang, Jiao
Li, Yingchun
coredata.dc:description: Biothiols play critical roles in maintaining physiological and pathological processes. However, effective and affordable tools for rapid and simple identification of biothiols are currently lacking. This work constructed a paper-based fluorescence sensor-array platform for biothiol recognition by grafting three biothiol receptors (TRs) modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on paper substrates. A multi-channel rotating paper-based microfluidic device (μPAD) was constructed by inkjet printing technology. The three TRs are self-synthesized Michael receptors, and they can trigger photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect to quench the fluorescence of CQDs. When biothiols were introduced onto the sensor array, they reacted with the CQDs-TRs hybrids at the sensor array due to their high nucleophilicity and disrupted the PET effect, resulting in partial recovery of the fluorescence. Six different biothiols produced their unique fluorescence response patterns as “fingerprints”, which can be quantitatively differentiated by linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA) in concentration ranges of 5∼100 μM. Practicality of the platform was verified by fast, accurate and sensitive identification of the six biothiols in blind samples and fetal bovine serum. In all, the platform features mini size, easy preparation and high integration, showing great potential for clinical point-of-care and other on-site testing towards multiple analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: μPADs
Sensor array
Carbon quantum dots
Michael acceptors
Biothiols

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Green and Sustainable Science and Technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction and utilization of the μPAD.
Characterization of materials and study of sensing Mechanism.
Fluorescence Response of CQDs-TR with Biothiols.
Identification of biothiols by Sensor-Array Platform.
Analysis of blind and real Samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CQDs. Inset is HRTEM image. (B) XRD spectrum of CQDs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs, and fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs under different excitation wavelengths. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs, CQDs-TR and CQDs-TR + GSH. (E) Fluorescence lifetime measurements of CQDs and CQDs-TR. (F) Principle of the PET mechanism between CQDs and TRs.

Fluorescence spectra of CQDs with different concentrations of (A) TR1, (B) TR2 and (C) TR3. Correlation plots of the fluorescence quenching efficiency against (D) TR1, (E) TR2, and (F) TR3 concentration. Error bars represent the standard deviation from three parallel tests.
Fluorescence spectra of CQDs-TR1 in the presence of (A) MPA, (B) Cys, (C) DTT, (D) DMSA, (E) GSH, and (F) Hcy with various concentrations.

(A) FTIR spectra of CQDs, CQDs/Paper and CQDs-TR/Paper. (B) Canonical score plot for the discrimination of the six biothiols at 10 μM. (C) Cluster heatmap and (D) box plot derived from fluorescence response for the discrimination of biothiols at 10 μM. (E) The canonical score plots for the discrimination of MPA at different concentrations. (F) Score plot of factor (1) versus the concentration of MPA. Error bars represent the standard deviation from three parallel tests.

(A) Canonical score and (B) HCA plots for the discrimination of MPA, DTT and GSH at various concentrations. MPA concentration: 10 μM, 50 μM and 100 μM; DTT concentration: 50 μM, 100 μM and 200 μM; GSH: 10 μM, 100 μM and 200 μM. (C) Canonical score and (D) HCA plots for the discrimination of DMSA/Cys mixture at 10 μM. (E) Canonical score for the discrimination of the six biothiols (10 µM) in FBS. (F) Canonical score plots for the discrimination of six biothiols (10 µM) and some interferents (50 µM).
Detection mechanism of biothiols based on PET of CQDs-TRs.
Schematic illustration of fabrication process of the fluorescence sensor-array platform for detection and discrimination of biothiols.

head.ce:title: A rotating paper-based microfluidic sensor array combining Michael acceptors and carbon quantum dots for discrimination of biothiols

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Science, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biothiols play critical roles in maintaining physiological and pathological processes. However, effective and affordable tools for rapid and simple identification of biothiols are currently lacking. This work constructed a paper-based fluorescence sensor-array platform for biothiol recognition by grafting three biothiol receptors (TRs) modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on paper substrates. A multi-channel rotating paper-based microfluidic device (μPAD) was constructed by inkjet printing technology. The three TRs are self-synthesized Michael receptors, and they can trigger photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect to quench the fluorescence of CQDs. When biothiols were introduced onto the sensor array, they reacted with the CQDs-TRs hybrids at the sensor array due to their high nucleophilicity and disrupted the PET effect, resulting in partial recovery of the fluorescence. Six different biothiols produced their unique fluorescence response patterns as “fingerprints”, which can be quantitatively differentiated by linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA) in concentration ranges of 5∼100 μM. Practicality of the platform was verified by fast, accurate and sensitive identification of the six biothiols in blind samples and fetal bovine serum. In all, the platform features mini size, easy preparation and high integration, showing great potential for clinical point-of-care and other on-site testing towards multiple analytes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biothiols such as cysteine, homocysteine and glutathione play crucial roles in various physiological functions and diseases due to their intrinsic redox and nucleophilic properties [1,2]. In general, abnormal in vivo biothiol levels are associated with many chronic and degenerative diseases such as cancer, Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disease, and neurodegenerative diseases [3–5]. Therefore, it is essential to monitor change of biothiols’ content in realms of medical research and disease diagnosis.

Currently, high performance liquid chromatography [6], mass spectrometry [7] and capillary electrophoresis [8] are routinely used for the quantitative detection and analysis of biothiols. Although these conventional instruments can achieve high accuracy and sensitivity, drawbacks such as time-consuming sample preparation, requirement of skilled operator and expensive instruments, impose limitations on their wide application in practical testing and point-of-care testing (POCT) [9,10]. Paper-based microfluidic devices (μPADs) with the virtues of simplicity, disposability, and low cost have been widely developed and applied in POCT [11,12]. Hydrophobic barriers were prepared on μPAD by printing to form hydrophilic regions. The sample can be spontaneously transported to the detection zone through the capillary force provided by the paper-based fibrous structure without any external driving force, enabling compact and rapid detection [13]. Till now, μPADs have been integrated with various detection techniques including colorimetry [14], electrochemistry [15], fluorescence [16], chemiluminescence [17], etc. Among them, benefiting from the merits of portability, integration, and sensitivity, fluorescence-based μPADs are gradually becoming a new development trend [18].

Recently researchers have worked to develop fluorescent sensors based on materials such as organic fluorophores [19], quantum dots [20] and metal nanoclusters[21] to achieve the sensitive detection of biothiols. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as zero-dimensional nanomaterials with sizes ranging from 1 to 10 nm, are a very ideal class of fluorescent sensing materials owing to the features of simple preparation, stable fluorescence, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [22,23]. However, because of the similarity of biothiols in structure and reactivity, most sensors can only measure total thiols, but it is difficult to selectively detect a single biothiol from them [24,25]. Therefore, designing sensors for the accurate discrimination of biothiols remains a difficult but important task, for which few examples have been reported.

Fortunately, the development of cross-reactive sensor arrays can address the above issues. Compared to conventional sensors with high selectivity, sensor arrays are constructed from a series of non-selective sensing units, which mimic the olfactory or gustatory system of mammals [26,27]. The sensor array generates non-specific responses to the analytes, and their responses are processed by multivariate analysis (linear discriminant analysis (LDA), hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA), or principal component analysis (PCA)) to obtain a unique fingerprint for the identification of target [28]. Sensor arrays eliminate the need of laborious synthesis of specific recognition receptors and offer advantages in terms of diversity, interference resistance, simultaneous detection, and discrimination between a range of compounds with similar structures or properties [29]. Sensor arrays that have been currently reported on the recognition of biothiols mainly exploit the high binding affinity between metal ions and biothiols, producing different fluorescence/colorimetric response patterns [30,31]. These studies have some shortcomings such as the lack of portability, and influences on accuracy from coexisting substances in biological samples that possess affinity with metal ions. Thus, it is imperative to develop a sensor array for the accurate identification of biothiols in biological samples.

Here, we combine paper-based microfluidics with array to construct a fluorescent sensing platform for rapid detection and identification of multiple biothiols. The–SH group in biothiols has strong nucleophilicity, which can react with Michael acceptors such as maleic anhydride and acryloyl groups by double bond addition reactions[32]. Based on this principle, three biothiols receptors (TRs) were designed, which were bound with blue-emitting CQDs through amidation reaction to construct a sensor array. TRs produce photo-induced electron transfer (PET) effect on CQDs and lead to fluorescence quenching, while the −SH group of thiols can destroy PET through addition reactions. Meanwhile, multi-channel rotating μPADs were constructed by inkjet printing technology, and equipment of a miniaturized fluorometer enables on-site monitoring. On the basis of this platform, the fluorescence response patterns of six biothiols were analyzed by LDA. Furthermore, the platform was successfully applied to differentiate these biothiols in real samples. To our knowledge, this is the first time that a fluorescent sensor-array platform based on PET is constructed for portable and rapid identification of biothiols, which provides a new avenue for POCT, disease diagnosis, and environmental monitoring.

Citric acid (CA), urea, sodium N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcy), mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), dithiothreitol (DTT) and dl-mercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and directly used without further purification. Dialysis membrane was ordered from Baoman biological technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai,China). Whatman glass fiber paper (GF/A18205) were supplied from Shanghai Jinpan biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained by ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan) and Infrared absorption spectrometer (Nicolet iS50, Thermo scientific, USA), respectively. The photoluminescence (PL) measurements were conducted with spectrofluometer (Fluoromax-4, HORIBA, French). The morphological information of CQDs was obtained through transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi, Japan). HP inkjet printer (Desk Jet 1112) was used to fabricate μPAD. Miniaturized fluorometer purchased from BLphotons precision optoelectronics Co., Ltd. (Suzhou, China).

The preparation of CQDs was performed by hydrothermal method [33]. Briefly, 1 g CA and 1 g urea were dissolved in 10 mL H2O, then the mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After that, the reaction solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove the solid. The supernatant was collected and added into dialysis membrane (MWCO of 500) for 48 h. Finally, the dialysis solution was freeze-dried to obtained solid.
Details of the synthesis of TRs can be found in the supporting information.

Firstly, CQDs was modified on the paper chip. Briefly, Φ 7 mm glass fiber paper was immersed in 0.2 M HCl solution for 30 min and washed with deionized water to remove excess HCl. Then, the paper was immersed in 20 mL of ethanol/water (1:1, v/v) containing 200 μL APTES for 4 h. After that, they were anchored on a petri dish to prevent floating. 1 mL CQDs (1 mg/mL), 5 mL NHS (50 mg/mL) and 5 mL EDC (25 mg/mL) were added and reacted with the paper in the dark for 6 h. Subsequently, CQDs/Paper was washed three times with deionized water. 1 mL TRs, 5 mL NHS and 5 mL EDC were added and reacted with CQDs/paper in the dark for 3 h. The obtained paper chips were denoted as CQDs-TR/Paper and stored at 4 °C when not in use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and utilization of the μPAD.
ce:section.ce:para: The μPADs developed in this study consists of filter paper, which was designed using Solid-Works 2014 3D CAD software (Concord, MA, U.S.A.), and hydrophobic borders were printed on filter paper by a low-cost, easy-to-operate inkjet printing technology (Supporting Information). The μPAD was composed of two layers. The bottom one was loaded with three paper chips modified with CQDs-TR. The upper layer was printed with sample inlet, observation port, exclusive channel and detection cell. It’s worth mentioning that the number of the arrays can be adjusted as required, and four arrays are printed for this experiment in order to improve the utilization of μPAD. Finally, the two layers are assembled and fixed with rivets to complete the construction of μPAD.

Biothiol solution was taken dropwise into the sample inlet and delivered to the detection cell for reaction by capillary action. The μPAD was placed into the miniaturized fluorometer and the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm was recorded. Six biothiols were tested five times with three sensor units to produce a training matrix of 6 × 3 × 5. Finally, LDA and HCA were performed by Origin® 2019b for the identification of biothiols. The binary and ternary mixtures of biothiols were tested using the similar approach with the total concentration of 10 μM. To identify unknown thiols, 48 unknown samples (10 μM) were randomly prepared. The raw data were then processed by LDA, and the results were compared with the training matrix to identify unknown thiols. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was applied as a real sample, which was diluted 50 times with DI water before testing to reduce matrix effects. Then, 10 μM of the six thiols were added to the FBS samples, and biothiols were measured using the same procedure as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of materials and study of sensing Mechanism.
ce:section.ce:para: To construct senor array for thiol detection, CQDs were first prepared by a hydrothermal method. The TEM image in Fig. 1 A shows that morphology of CQDs is uniform spherical with sizes around 5 nm. High-resolution TEM image reveals the interlayer lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which represents the diffraction planes of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1A, Inset). The XRD spectrum of CQDs shows a broad peak centered at around 27°, which is attributed to the (002) planes of graphite (Fig. 1B). As shown in Fig. 1C, the UV–vis spectrum demonstrates that CQDs have a wide absorption band in a range of 300∼380 nm., the position of emission band almost doesn’t change with the excitation wavelength changing, but the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm increased from the excitation wavelength of 310 nm to 330 nm, and then decreased with the excitation wavelength continuously increasing. The results indicate that the emission wavelength of CQDs is completely independent of the excitation wavelength, but the intensity relies on it and the optimal excitation wavelength is 330 nm. As shown in Fig S1, CQDs emit strong blue light under UV lamp irradiation, of which fluorescence quantum yield is 48 %. The calculation formula of quantum yield was given in Supporting Information. The results confirmed that CQDs possess excellent photoluminescence performance.

Among the various Michael receptors, maleimides, nitroolefins, and cyanoolefins are very attractive electrophilic groups. Therefore, three TRs were prepared by modifying the above three groups on the carboxyphenyl ring. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the detection mechanism is that TRs produce a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) effect on CQDs, which turns off the fluorescence of CQDs. In the presence of biothiols, their -SH groups react with TRs through Michael addition reaction, disrupting the PET effect and turning on the fluorescence of CQDs.

The chemical structures of TRs were confirmed by FTIR spectra, 1H NMR and 13C NMR. The results proved the successful synthesis of TRs and the absence of impurities (Fig. S2 and S3). Subsequently, CQDs and CQDs-TR1 were analyzed with XPS (Fig. S4). Obvious increase of the proportion of N element and amide group after CQDs modified with TR1 was found (Table S1 and S2), verifying the successful preparation of CQD-TR1.

As we expected, the emission peak of CQDs decreased significantly with the addition of TRs, while the subsequent introduction of GSH led to the recovery of fluorescence intensity (Fig. 1D). To investigate the mechanism of TRs turning off CQD fluorescence, spectroscopic, fluorescence lifetime and electrochemical characterization were performed. Firstly, the overlap between absorption band of the quencher and the excitation and/or emission band of the fluorophore spectra was discussed. Fig. S5A shows that the absorption bands of TRs are located in a range of 280∼360 nm, which overlaps the fluorescence excitation wavelength (330 nm) rather than emission band (370∼550 nm) of CQDs. These results imply that the internal filtration effect (IFE) rather than fluorescence resonance energy transfer makes TRs have inhibitory influence on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. Next, the fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs and CQDs-TRs were measured. As shown in Fig. 1E, their decay curves were well fitted with the double exponential decay function. The average lifetime of CQDs and the three CQDs-TRs is 6.6 ns, 5.71 ns, 4.47 ns and 5.96 ns, respectively. The phenomenon of reduced fluorescence lifetime indicates an energy/electron transfer process between CQDs and TRs. Thus, the possible inhibitory mechanism of TRs on CQDs may be photoinduced electron transfer (PET). To better understand the PET process, highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbit (LUMO) energy levels of CQDs and TRs were obtained with the assistant of electrochemical measurements and UV–vis spectra. The energy of HOMO and LUMU is determined by the following empirical equation: E HOMO (eV) = −(E ox + 4.74), E LUMO = E HOMO + E g, where E ox and E g is the first oxidation potential and electronic band gap, respectively [34]. As shown in Fig. S5B and C, the E g and E ox for CQDs and TRs were acquired from Taus plots and cyclic voltammograms [35]. Consequently, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of CQDs and TRs were calculated and presented in Fig. 1F. As seen from this, PET could occur from CQDs to TRs, which resulted in fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of CQDs by TRs stems from the synergistic effect of IFE and PET.

It's worth noting that the fluorescence cannot return to the initial value after adding biothiol even if its amount reaches saturation. Fig. S5A shows that the position of absorption bands for biothiol-TRs is negatively shifted a little, indicating IFE doesn’t attenuate. Thus, the increment in intensity may be attributed to the fact that the electrophilic groups (maleimides, olefine) in TRs react with the −SH group of GSH through Michael addition reaction, which causes damage to PET effect. In order to exclude the factor of the turning on effect of CQDs directly caused by biothiols, the interaction between CQDs and biothiols without TRs was investigated. As shown in Fig. S6, by mixing CQDs with biothiols, there are negligible decrements in fluorescence responses compared with CQDs alone, indicating that biothiols mainly react with the TRs to affect the fluorescence of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Response of CQDs-TR with Biothiols.
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, the sensor array in this work is “signal off–on” type. The fluorescence of CQDs is quenched after the introduction of TRs, and the addition of biothiol restores the fluorescence. Therefore, it is critical for the sensor array to select an appropriate concentration of TRs. As shown in Fig. 2 , the fluorescence intensity of CQDs gradually decreased with the increase of concentration of TRs, indicating that the three TRs could effectively quench the CQDs fluorescence. Compared with the three TRs at the same concentration, the quenching effect of TR3 was the most effective. The optimal concentrations of the three TRs were set as 2.0 mM, 1.8 mM and 1.2 mM, respectively, at which the quenching response reached about 90 %.

To quantitatively study the interaction between the TRs and biothiols, six biothiols including MPA, Cys, DTT, DMSA, Hcy and GSH were chosen as analytical models and a fluorescence titration method was adopted. As shown in Fig. 3 , as the concentration of biothiols increasing, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-TR1 increased gradually. Different biothiols possess different inhibitory degrees on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-TR1. The other probes (CQDs-TR2 and CQDs-TR3) also displayed the similar phenomenon after biothiols addition (Fig. S7, S8). The above results convey that −SH groups in the six biothiols can react with electrophilic groups in TRs by Michael addition reaction to disrupt PET effects between CQDs and TRs. Diversity of electrophilicity in TRs and diversity of nucleophilicity in biothiols make each TRs react with the six biothiols at different rates and produce different fluorescence responses. Therefore, despite the similarities in structure and reactivity of these biothiols, it is possible to construct sensor arrays for recognition of biothiols utilizing the differences in the reactions of the three TRs with biothiols.

Aiming at an efficient, fast and economical detection of biothiols, a new sensor-array platform was designed based on the diversity in the reaction between the three TRs and the six biothiols, which consists of three main components: μPAD, an integrated fluorescent detector and a computer for signal processing. The schematic diagram of this multifunctional inspection platform is shown in Scheme 2 , and the photo is shown in Fig S9. The fluorescent detector is assembled with the μPAD by 3D printing the test stand to enable the detection of samples in a dark environment. To increase the flexibility of the μPAD, three CQDs-TRs were decorated on a tailored paper chip that were assembled on the μPAD as sensing units. The detection port of the detector corresponds to the observation port of the μPAD. As the sample solution is dropped into the inlet, it is transported to the detection cell by capillary action and permeates to the paper chip below the detection cell to react.

The modification process of the paper chip was shown in Scheme 2. First, the paper chip was immersed in HCl solution to remove impurities and activate the hydroxyl groups on the surface of the glass fiber. Then, the paper chip was treated with APTES, during which triethoxysilane groups in APTES reacted with surface silanol groups (-Si-OH). Such treatment allowed for modification of the cellulose surface with amino groups. Under the catalysis of EDS/NHS, the paper chip was first grafted with CQDs having a large number of carboxyl functional groups by covalent binding, followed by decoration of the three TRs on the surface of CQDs. The paper chip began with non-fluorescent, then fluorescence appeared, and finally the fluorescence was quenched (Fig S10). FTIR spectra in Fig. 4
A show that the peaks of CQDs at 3416 cm−1 and 3178 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibration of −NH and −OH, respectively. Meanwhile, the peak at 1716 cm−1 stems from the stretching vibration of −CO, indicating that the CQDs surface contains a large number of carboxyl and amino groups. Since glass fiber has a large amount of SiO2, the characteristic peaks of CQDs/Paper at 468 cm−1 and 785 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of the Si −O bond, and the peak at 1066 cm−1 is generated by the antisymmetric stretching vibration of the Si−O−Si bond. The peaks located at 1652 cm−1and 1712 cm−1 are respectively ascribed to stretching vibration of −N H and −CO, which implies successful grafting of CQDs on the paper surface. For CQDs-TR/Paper, enhancement of −N H and −CO characteristic peaks at 1652 cm−1and 1712 cm−1 suggests the formation of amide bound, indicating that the recognition molecules were successfully modified on CQDs/paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of biothiols by Sensor-Array Platform.
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the previous discussion, the fluorescence reaction mechanism between biothiols and CQDs-TR was the PET effect. To investigate the fluorescence response of the sensor-array platform to the six biothiols, biothiols at a concentration of 10 μM were reacted with each of the three sensing units and measured by an integrated fluorescence detector. These differences in fluorescence intensity changes ((F −F 0)/F) can be used to identify biothiols. A fluorescence response matrix consisting of 3 paper chips × 6 biothiols × 5 replicates were built. Multivariate analysis methods (LDA and HCA) convert complex multidimensional data into simple visualization patterns. As shown in Fig. 4B, the 30 (6 biothiols × 5 replicates) typical fluorescence response patterns were divided into six different groups with no overlap, indicating that the sensor array has good resolution of biothiols. A cluster heatmap was obtained based on the fluorescence intensity response of the sensor array to the six biothiols (Fig. 4C). The cluster results show that all biothiols were correctly assigned to their respective groups. From the heatmap, the reaction of each sensing unit with biothiols can be clearly seen. For example, for the CQDs-TR1/Paper sensing unit, the fluorescence intensities of GSH, HCY and MPA vary low, while for the CQDs-TR3/Paper sensing unit, those for DTT and MPA vary much. The box plots also depict the difference in responses of the three sensing units for each biothiols as follows: DTT > GSH > Hcy > MPA > Cys > DMSA (Fig. 4D). The recognition behavior of the platform was compared with other reported methods, and the results showed that the developed platform has good recognition capability (Table S3).

Next, the identification ability of the array for different concentrations of an individual biothiol was examined. Fig. 4E shows the discrimination performance of μPAD for different concentrations (5∼100 μM) of MPA. It can be seen that MPA can be well differentiated at all the concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4F, the canonical factor 1 is linearly related to the concentration, indicating that the interaction between the target biothiol and the array was homogeneous and stable. Analogously, differentiation experiments for the other biothiols (Cys, DTT, DMSA, Hcy and GSH) were also conducted at the same conditions. The results show that the platform can clearly distinguish different concentrations of a single biothiol (Fig. S11–S13). The platform was also examined for the differentiation of MPA, GSH and DTT at different concentrations. As illustrated in Fig. 5A, B, the LDA and HCA plots for different concentrations of the three biothiols display no significant overlap between them, which could be clearly distinguished.

Besides, the ability of the array to discriminate mixtures of binary and ternary (total concentration of 10 μM) was assessed. Fig. 5C shows that the platform successfully distinguished the mixtures of DMSA and Cys at different ratios, and the two factors exhibit eximious clustering properties with a total variance dispersion of 99.92 %. All 25 cases (5 mixtures with 5 replicates) were exactly assigned to their individual groups (Fig. 5D). Similarly, ternary mixtures were well distinguished in the LDA and HCA plots (Fig. S14). The interference of other substances including Na+, K+, Ca+, Cl– and NO3 –was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 5F, all interfering substances are well separated from the six biothiol clusters, suggesting that the sensor array has significant anti-interference capability. Repeatability and long-term stability of the platform were also evaluated. As shown in Fig. S15, there is no significant decrease in sensing responses. In addition, the cost of the constructed device was relatively low, offering a significant price advantage compared to commercial kits. These results indicate that the device is highly feasible for identifying biothiols in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of blind and real Samples.
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality of the platform, individual biothiols and their binary and ternary mixtures (total concentration of 10 μM) were used as blind samples. As listed in Table S4, 48 blind samples were accurately identified. By comparing the performance of some other reported sensor arrays, our device demonstrated comparable accuracy in blind sample testing (Table S5). To further explore the application of the device in real samples, FBS was employed. As shown in Fig. 5E, these six biothiols are separated entirely in FBS. The above results imply that the platform can identify the six biothiols well in real samples.

In this study, a fluorescent sensor-array platform was developed for the identification of biothiols. CQDs served as the fluorophore and three self-synthesized Michael receptors were selected as TRs. The binding of the TRs and the CQDs produces PET effect and therefore induces fluorescence quenching. The PET process was disrupted by the reaction between biothiols and TRs to partially restore fluorescence. Recognition patterns of different biothiols were established using the variation of fluorescence intensity. In addition, multi-channel rotating μPAD with low cost and high efficiency merits was built by facile inkjet printing technology. The sensor array was incorporated with the μPAD and equipped with a miniaturized fluorometer for on-site detection. The LDA and HCA results showed that the platform is capable of discriminating single and mixed biothiols with 100 % accuracy. The practical application of the platform was further validated by the successful discrimination of blind and real samples. The platform can effectively solve the problems of cumbersome operation, large instrument size and high cost. As an ideal field-testing tool, it is expected to play an important role in disease diagnosis, daily physiological monitoring, environmental pollution detection, food safety assessment, etc.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (81973280, 82104121, 82173778), Key Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shenzhen (JCYJ20200109113410174), and Shenzhen Science and Technology Program (KQTD20170810105439418).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.140065.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 2
